---
title: "Procedure"
---

[← Back to Lab 2 overview](index.qmd)

<!-- Internal: this procedure is being refined as the lab runs;
     expect day-to-day updates this quarter. -->

- The lab collects **six step-response datasets** per team — three parameter configurations (Baseline, Feedforward, Tuned) × two control channels (speed, yaw-rate).
- The same field workflow runs three times; only the parameter set changes between cases.
- **Quality is checked at the field** before each parameter-set change, so problems are caught while the boat is still in the water.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**Plan for two field sessions.** Getting clean step responses on the first day is rare — the first session typically surfaces issues (vehicle setup, parameter staging, pilot/scribe rhythm) that need fixing before clean data lands on a second day. Build in time between sessions for laptop-side processing.
:::

## 1. Pre-lab preparation

### 1.1 Day before

- Collect: Boat, 900 MHz radio, R/C transmitter, ground laptop (with Mission Planner).
- Charge: Batteries for vehicle propulsion, autopilot, R/C transmitter, ground laptop.

### 1.2 Day of

- **Test card / data sheet printed** — one row per dataset (six total) for parameter values, observations, and quality-check verdict.
- **Parameter files staged on the laptop.** You are given the **baseline** `.param` for the **Base** experiment.
- **Roles:**
  - **Planner** — drives Mission Planner: monitors ARM and mode state, watches the real-time tuning graphs, and saves a timestamped parameter export at the start of each run.
  - **Scribe** — keeps the test card and records UTC time + intent at every arming event. The scribe's notes are what makes the resulting tens of `.mat` files navigable during post-processing.
  - **Pilot** — drives the boat with the RC transmitter and executes the step inputs that exercise the closed-loop controllers.

## 2. Shore setup

Steps to confirm at the launch site, before any data collection.

- Establish telemetry link with Mission Planner.
- Load the baseline `.param` file and confirm the relevant `ATC_*` parameters are at the expected values. See the [Stabilization Layer](../../autopilot/stabilization.qmd) reference for what each parameter does.
- Confirm you can ARM and switch from MANUAL to ACRO modes.
- You may need to re-bind the RC transmitter and/or calibrate sensors.

## 3. Setup and execute experiments

The lab cycles through three experiments — **Base**, **FeedForward**, **Tuned** — applying the same procedure to each. Only the parameter set changes between cases (§3.4).

### 3.1 Per-arm rhythm and logging

The autopilot starts a **new log file every time the pilot arms it**. Treat each arm-disarm cycle as one experimental purpose — one cycle for the speed step trials, one for the yaw-rate trials. This keeps each `.mat` file scoped to a single channel, which makes the MATLAB time-series easier to inspect. Expect to accumulate dozens of `.mat` files over a session; the **scribe's notes — UTC time (with seconds) plus a brief intent description at every arm** — are how you map each `.mat` back to what was being tested. The post-processed `.mat` filename includes the UTC timestamp.

### 3.2 Procedure for each experiment

Run the following sequence three times, once per experiment (Base → FeedForward → Tuned).

#### Set up the experiment

1. **Change the parameter set** for this experiment via Mission Planner (Config → Full Parameter Tree). For **Base**, load the baseline `.param`. For **FeedForward** and **Tuned**, load the edited copies you prepared during [pre-lab prep](#day-of).
2. **Verify the relevant `ATC_*` parameters** at the vehicle. Mission Planner shows live values — see the [Stabilization Layer](../../autopilot/stabilization.qmd) reference for what each parameter does and which ones change between experiments.

#### Speed (throttle) step run — one arm-disarm cycle

3. Pilot brings the USV to **open water** and to **rest**.
4. **Planner saves the current parameter file** to the laptop with a timestamp in the filename (Mission Planner → Config → Full Parameter Tree → Save). Pilot then **disarms and re-arms** (closes the previous log, opens a fresh one). Scribe records UTC time + intent (e.g. *"Base, surge step"*).
5. Pilot switches the autopilot into **ACRO** mode. **Planner confirms ACRO** in Mission Planner before the pilot proceeds.
6. **Repeat 3–5 times:**
   - Apply a step throttle command at **~50% of full stick deflection** (mid-range — keeps the actuator out of saturation while staying visible above noise).
   - **Hold for 20–30 s** so the surge response settles into a clear steady value.
   - Return throttle to zero; allow the boat to come back to rest before the next repeat.
7. Pilot **disarms** to close the speed-trial log.

#### Yaw-rate (steering) step run — one arm-disarm cycle

8. Pilot brings the boat to a **low, approximately constant surge speed** — typically around **~50% throttle** so the rudder has flow but the boat isn't fighting acceleration during the test. (At zero surge speed a steering command does nothing useful — the rudder needs forward water flow to produce a turning moment.)
9. **Planner saves the current parameter file** with a timestamp. Pilot then **disarms and re-arms**. Scribe records UTC time + intent (e.g. *"Base, yaw step"*).
10. Pilot switches the autopilot into **ACRO** mode. **Planner confirms ACRO** before the pilot proceeds.
11. **Repeat 3–5 times, alternating direction** (positive then negative stick deflection so any asymmetry is visible):
    - Apply a step steering command at roughly **~50% rudder**. Use the **same step fraction** in every experiment (A, B, C) so the metrics are directly comparable.
    - **Hold until the yaw rate settles** to a steady value.
    - Scribe records the surge speed at the time of each step, the step fraction, and the direction.
12. Pilot **disarms** to close the yaw-trial log.

#### Field quality check (before changing parameters or leaving the field)

13. Pull both `.BIN` logs (SD card or via Mission Planner). Convert each with [`bin2mat.py`](https://github.com/bsb808/introduction-to-feedback-control/blob/main/utils/ardu_utils/bin2mat.py).
14. Plot in MATLAB with [`proc_closedloop_response.m`](https://github.com/bsb808/introduction-to-feedback-control/blob/main/book/w07_lab_usv_pid/code/proc_closedloop_response.m).
15. For each step in the figure, verify:
    - **Setpoint reaches the commanded value** (no rate-limited ramp instead of a step).
    - **Measured response settles** to a steady value within ~10 s.
    - **Control effort is not pinned** at ±1 for an extended period (saturation).
    - **PIDA / PIDS log channels are present** (PID-internal logging is on; `PIDA` for speed, `PIDS` for yaw-rate — the naming is counterintuitive).
    - **No long gaps** in the GPS or STER sample stream.
16. **If a check fails → recollect that run before changing parameters or moving on.** If it's a parameter or vehicle issue → resolve, then continue.
17. Save and label both `.mat` files on the test card: filename, parameter values, quality-check verdict.

### 3.3 What changes between experiments

| Experiment | Parameter changes from previous | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Base** | Lab preset configuration; ArduRover stock PID gains; FF off; rate limiters off; throttle baseline off | First case — fully load the lab baseline `.param` |
| **FeedForward** | Restore `ATC_SPEED_FF` and `ATC_STR_RAT_FF` to lab values | Same PID gains as Base |
| **Tuned** | Adjust `ATC_SPEED_P/I/D` and `ATC_STR_RAT_P/I/D` for best response | FF kept on. Use Mission Planner's real-time tuning graphs to iterate on candidate gains; only execute the full arm-disarm logging cycle (steps 3–17) for the gain values you decide to keep. |

### 3.4 Common gotchas

- **Don't change parameters while ARMed.** Parameter changes get baked into the active log in a way that makes it hard to verify what was active when. Disarm, change, save the new param file, then re-arm.
- **It's easy to forget ACRO.** A test in MANUAL looks identical to one in ACRO from the pilot's perspective, but the closed-loop controllers aren't engaged. The planner's ACRO confirmation in step 5 / step 10 is the safety net — don't skip it.
- **Inter-step gap too short.** Between repeats within a run, allow the response to fully settle (return to rest, or to baseline yaw rate) before the next step. A short gap mixes one step's tail into the next step's start.
- **Saturation.** If the control effort pins at ±1 (full PWM) for an extended period, what you're measuring is the *saturated* dynamics, not the linear closed-loop response. Drop the step magnitude — or, in **Tuned**, gain authority through tuning.
- **Same step fraction across Base / FeedForward / Tuned.** Comparing rise time, overshoot, and steady-state error across experiments only makes sense if the input is the same magnitude every time.
- **One channel per arm-disarm cycle.** Don't intermingle speed and yaw steps in the same log — it makes the MATLAB inspection harder than it needs to be.
- **Two timezones.** ArduPilot logs and the post-processed `.mat` filenames are in **UTC**. Scribe notes are in local time. The post-processing step adds the UTC stamp to the filename automatically; the scribe should record UTC at the field too so the two match without arithmetic later.

## 4. End-of-day

Wrap up the field session.

- All six `.mat` files transferred to the team's shared location.
- Test card filled in (one row per dataset, with quality-check verdict).
- Vehicle returned to nominal parameter configuration (rate limiters restored, FF restored, throttle baseline restored) — load a `.param` file with the post-lab nominal values.
- Battery on charger, vehicle stowed.

## 5. Hand-off to next week

Datasets and notes are the input to the Week-8 analysis: process each `.mat` with [`closedloop_stepinfo.m`](https://github.com/bsb808/introduction-to-feedback-control/blob/main/book/w07_lab_usv_pid/code/closedloop_stepinfo.m) to extract rise time, settling time, overshoot, and steady-state error, then compare across the three experiments.
